---
title: "Open Data: The One Cause"
description: "Canada Observatory's single exception to 'no advocacy' — the creation, use, and open availability of public data"
---

Canada Observatory does not take policy positions. We don't say whether taxes are too
high, immigration too fast, or health spending too low — we show the numbers,
the peer comparison, and the official benchmark, and leave the judgment to you.

We make **one deliberate exception**: we advocate for the **creation, use, and
open availability of high-quality public data**. We hold this position openly
because the project cannot pretend to be neutral about its own foundation.
Every chart on this site exists because a public agency counted something
carefully and published the result for anyone to use. No open data, no Canada Observatory
— and, more importantly, no shared factual ground for the country's arguments.

## Why it matters

The case is an old and broadly shared one — it is the logic of the census
itself, of public audit, and of open government, supported across the political
spectrum:

- **Transparency.** Citizens can only evaluate how the country is doing — and
  hold institutions to account — if the measuring is done professionally and
  the results are published for everyone, not summarized selectively.
- **Informed civic engagement.** Disagreement is healthy; arguing from
  different facts is corrosive. A shared, citable evidence base doesn't end
  debates, but it lets them be about values and trade-offs rather than about
  whose numbers are real.
- **Value.** Data collected once at public expense gets used thousands of
  times — by researchers, journalists, businesses, community groups, and
  sites like this one — but only if it is open, machine-readable, and free.

## What we mean, concretely

**Creation.** Statistical agencies need the mandate, independence, and funding
to measure the country rigorously and continuously. Canada is fortunate:
Statistics Canada is internationally respected, and the census is the
backbone of almost everything subnational on this site. That quality is a
choice that has to keep being made. The clearest illustration of the stakes is
in our own charts: the 2011 census cycle replaced the mandatory long-form
census with a voluntary survey, response rates fell, and the data quality
suffered — which is why, wherever 2011 appears in our census-based series, we
flag it as the lower-quality point in the line. When collection weakens, the
gap never fully heals.

**Open access.** Publication should be the default: machine-readable formats,
open licences, stable links, free of charge. Canada has made real strides —
the Open Government Licence, Statistics Canada's bulk tables and APIs,
Environment and Climate Change Canada's indicator program, the Bank of
Canada's Valet API, and municipal open-data portals like the one this site's
Toronto crime maps are built on. Where data exists but sits behind
restrictive licensing, the public conversation is poorer for it.

**Use.** Data only matters when someone uses it. The final step of openness
is making data *usable* — and used: cleaned, documented, visualized, taught.
That is the part CanObs tries to contribute. We aggregate scattered official
sources into one consistent, continually updated reference, cite everything,
and publish our cleaned datasets and code so anyone can check or build on our
work.

## What this exception is not

It is not a back door to other positions. Advocating for good measurement
does not tell you what the measurements should make you conclude. We hold
ourselves to that line: when a dataset has limitations, we say so on the
chart; when a source is discontinued or degraded, we note it rather than
quietly substituting something weaker; and we do not grade governments on
anything — including this.

## If you agree

Use the data. Cite it — every chart here links to its source. Download
[our cleaned CSVs](https://github.com/CanadaObservatory/CanadaDataObs) and
build something. Answer the census like it matters, because it does. And when
public data helps you understand something, tell people where the numbers
came from — the habit of citing sources is most of what "informed civic
conversation" means.
